sentences.text: I told you, from the mid of March until the end of April.
Well, I don't remember anymore when Prague was liberated.
There was an uprising in Prague, and hundreds got killed while the Nazis were still there.
I think it was either the end of April, or the first days of May.
It was before the-- the-- the war would end on the ninth of May.
But it was either the third or fourth of May, something like this.
I don't remember anymore.
Russians.
Russians.
Let me tell you how-- how this happened, the ninth.
This was a small town, where I was.
As a matter of fact, and another interesting thing, when they heard the henchman there, all the young people want to learn French.
I give the lecture, lessons in French.
And they paid me with food.
Honest to the goodness.
It-- it-- it was-- it was a very historic town.
You had one of the most famous composers in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in Czechoslovakia was Smetana.
As a matter of fact, most people don't know 'The HaTikvah' comes from-- from a symphony of Smetana.
And-- and-- and-- and-- the-- the-- the symphony's called 'Ma Vlast.' And Smetana left in this-- lived in this town.
[NON-ENGLISH], the Vltava goes there.
It's a very idyllic town.
Beautiful, beautiful.
And--
Yeah, yeah, the Moldau.
Yeah, right, yeah.
The Moldau.
And-- and-- and-- and Smetana wrote the symphony right in this town.
They showed me where he lived there.
So--
One day we suddenly-- when something happens in a small town, everyone knows about it.
There was a group of-- a company of SS.
The SS was retreating from Czechoslovakia.
And they got lost somehow.
They must have been young kids, 16, 17, 18 years old, heavily armed.
And they lost their way.
And they came into town.
And 15 years later, Russians came and were chasing them.
They somehow spotted them either by air or what have you.
And they caught them all, and they slaughtered them like animals.
And-- and they-- they wanted me also to shoot them.
I said no, I cannot do that.
But Czech women, girls, they shot them.
Russians, they said run.
And they shot them mercilessly.
They had suffered so much.
I can understand it.
And it-- it was fresh, then, in their memory.
And-- and the Russians came, very primitive.
They were either from Kyrgyzstan or-- or-- or Uzbekistan.
They looked, all, Mongolian.
Like mongols.
They didn't have, even, shoes.
I'm telling you, they were dressed so pitifully, that's the word.
Yeah, yeah, uniforms.
Yeah.
But it-- it-- not even-- regular shoes, they had.
It was terrible.
Yeah, yeah.
But they had-- they had-- heavily armed.
They all had Kalashnikovs.
You know, those-- those light machine guns.
And they took over the town.
And when they were there, I stayed there another day or two.
And then I said, I have to go back to France.
And even at that time, for some reason, I didn't have the guts to tell him them the truth.
I don't know.
I [INAUDIBLE] I-- I-- I-- it would be so disappoint them that I lied to them.
They were so good to me.
They treated me like a son, literally.
And I didn't have the heart to tell them.
I went to Prague.
They told me, if you want to be evacuated to-- to France, the best thing is go to-- go to Prague.
So I went, of course, by-- by-- by-- by-- they call this tramping.
I mean, I-- I stopped the truck.
Yeah, yeah.
That's exactly right.
And I arrived.
It took me a day or two.
And then in Prague, there was, already, UNRRA.
That's this organization of United Nations was already organizing there.
Oh, there-- there were tens of thousands of refugees from all over the world.
And believe it or not, we all slept in-- in the-- in the big palace-- of [NON-ENGLISH], the palace of the kings.
Of course, on the floor we slept.
But that besides the point.
And I tried to go-- to go to France.
They didn't let me.
They didn't trust me.
They interrogate me, the UNRRA people.
They were Frenchmen.
They said, you tell me a story.
I don't believe that.
I-- I-- I went from there.
First of all, again, the railways were interrupted.
Every five miles was destroyed by-- by-- by the American or the-- the Russian bombers.
And I went first to Krakow.
And I went to find out if some of my friends survived.
Because they were from Krakow, what I knew in the camp.
And I want to find out, maybe my sister's alive anywhere.
Because at that time, in Poland, the Jewish committees were already working that anybody that arrived put theirs name down.
And they had lists.
And-- and they could call up, even, by phone.
If-- if-- it was possible to get phone connections.
And I went into a man that was a member of the historical commission.
He was a Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] [?
Wolff, ?] an intellectual, a writer.
He was a writer before the war.
And I lived with him for a week.
And then I said, I have to look further.
I have to go back to Germany.
Maybe my sister survived.
She was at an age that she had a chance to survive.
And I went to Brno.
Brno, that is in Czechoslovakia, where the American troops stopped.
And I went to the American-- to-- to-- to-- to the-- where the Americans were stationed.
And they were two Jewish cooks that I recognize right away, they are Jewish.
And they spoke Yiddish.
So I spoke to them.
And they gave me what they call a GI uniform.
And they fed me.
I got food.
Hey, I got sick, so much food, I got.
And then they said, if you want to go back to your hometown, we give you an American transport that goes through the Russian zone.
Because Germany was then split.
They had to go through the Russian zone to get to the Western zone, to the American occupied zone.
And I got back to-- to-- to Germany.
And then, in-- in-- in my hometown, I found out that there is a former farm that belonged to a Jewish Zionist organization, where people were trained for going to Palestine.
And that is occupied by Jews.
That's what they told me.
So I made my way, and [INAUDIBLE] there.
And what do I find out?
There were about 8 or 10 of my friends from Brno, from the camp, that were among the Zionist groups.
They were loaded on the-- on the-- on the cattle cars, and went to Buchenwald.
They arrived in Buchenwald.
And then they were liberated.
Buchenwald was liberated by the American army.
And they called themselves-- they made a big sign on the main building, 'Kibbutz Buchenwald.' And-- and-- and-- and more and more people found out about it.
And at the end there were about 150 youngsters.
And we all trained.
We worked on the farm for a few months to learn and to go to Israel, to Palestine at that time, and to live in a kibbutz.
In the meantime, while I was there, I find out from a friend in Hamburg that a school teacher from a Jewish school in Gelsenkirchen found out that my sister is alive in Dantzic.
I didn't believe it.
How is that possible?
I went.
I said to my kibbutz, I'm sorry.
I have to go to find my sister.
And I went to Hamburg.
He was in charge of-- of-- of-- of a-- like the Salvation Army had when new refugees came.
And one of the biggest cigarette companies in Germany was Haus [GERMAN], like here, Marlboro.
And that was a building that he confiscated from the Germans.
And they had about 150 refugees living there.
They had a lot of rooms there, where it used to be bureaus before, now they make us sleeping rooms there.
And he told me it's true.
That the wife of the Cantor, not the schoolteacher.
And she was in Dantzic.
And she knows that your sister is living in Dantzic.
So what did I do?
Have to go to Dantzic.
You have no papers.
And you have to go to the police zone, to the Russian zone.
I-- I decided, I have to find her.
And I went first to Berlin.
And in Berlin, just arrived a transfer from Dantzic, of half Jews.
They-- they-- the Nazis kept them there.
They were mixed-- mixed marriages.
You know?
They had, sometimes, privilege.
It depend, really, on the local mayor.
If he didn't like them, he sent them to-- both to Auschwitz.
If he liked him, he left them there.
Because he was-- so to be protected.
Because they were-- the husbands were-- were-- were Germans.
Or their wives were Germans.
And they worked in the local industries.
They were semi-prisoners also, like this.
And he told me, yeah, this true.
There is a-- Elly Diament is in-- in-- in Dantzic.
So--
How I got there-- how I got-- ah, I played dumb and deaf because the Russians, if you had a number and you speak only German, you are an SS man that made a number.
And first they shoot and then they ask questions.
So I had to play a role.
I-- I-- I played deaf and dumb.
I never showed them that I'm-- that-- that I'm a refugee, that I'm a survivor.
And-- and-- and I got, at night, to Dantzic.
And at-- at the railway-- everything at the center of the town was the railway station.
Because that was the only place that-- not-- even if it was destroyed, they rebuilt it.
Because it was important for communication.
And there were two Jews.
They-- they were doing business with the Russians, hard currency.
Marks and dollars and-- and-- and-- and rubles.
And-- and-- and zloties.
And there was an agreement if you want to know if somebody is Jewish, you-- you said to him [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] means our-- our people, in-- in-- in Yiddish, in Hebrew.
And I ask one guy that looked like a real shrewd, shrewd merchant.
He was dealing and wheeling, and dealing, don't ask me, with everybody.
He said, I don't know about your sister.
But I know where the committee-- we call it the committee, the-- the Jewish committee, where it is.
And we went there.
It must have been three o'clock in the morning.
And let me tell you something.
There were Russian patrols in the city of Dantzic, all night.
And they were the most dangerous people because they-- when they saw somebody, exactly.
Anybody they saw in the street, was-- there was strict-- strict curfew.
Well, it was-- was either a terrorist, a German or SS man, what have you .
It took us three hours, walking in hiding until we arrived at the committee.
He-- he knew where it was.
My luck, a sore muzzle.
We knocked at the door.
He didn't want to open. he was afraid that somebody comes marauding there.
Finally opened.
He He yeah.
And he explained to us where they live.
She live with three other girls that were together with her in concentration camp, Sutthof.
They were liberated together and they became like sisters.
They lived in-- in Wernau.
And they also were scared.
Because at night, they were breaking into-- into homes and raping survivors, Jewish, they didn't give a-- the Russians didn't give a damn.
There was no discipline.
They were like animals.
Everybody knows that.
So anyway, this man was so loyal to me.
We again-- it was very dangerous-- went at night.
And we finally came.
And they had a little restaurant.
A restaurant, with some-- some-- some-- some food, where-- very primitive.
And we knocked at the door at night.
And-- and-- and my sister was already married.
It's a Polish-Jewish guy.
And he said, I will never forget, in Polish, [POLISH], who is there?
And I said, in-- in German.
And my sister heard my voice after I haven't seen her for five years.
She got hysteric, started crying.
That's my brother.
That's my brother.
Oh my god.
And they opened up.
And here she was.
She couldn't believe it.
She was hysterical.
We all cried. yeah, that's where I found here.
He-- he--
I-- I-- I-- from-- from-- from people who were together with them.
You see?
The only way to find out is, for example, if you find somebody from my hometown, they was together, he could tell you .
You know-- you know Sigi [INAUDIBLE].
It's [INAUDIBLE], yeah?
She knew my mother.
They were together in Riga.
Yeah, they knew each other.
Yeah.
They were also from the same-- also from Westphalia.
So they kept in sort of-- more or less, together.
That the only way to find out is so people were together.
And-- and they were together on the death march.
They were together there.
And they knew about it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No, I-- I heard, already, from Riga, the big transport came to Auschwitz.
And I talked to a man that was, after the selection of Dr.
Mengele, was assigned to work.
Because he was still in good shape.
And he told me there were several transports from Riga.
The women and the children went right away, in the gas chambers.
So--
That's-- that's a story too.
While I was still in the kibbutz, Buchenwald, in this farm, we decided that we have to go to other DP camps and get more youngsters that used to be in Zionist youth movement.
Because we wanted to have a shipful.
We want to go, illegal, to Israel.
That It was our plan, already, then.
And they sent me to-- I was one of the organizers.
They sent me to Bergen-Belsen concentration camp.
And in Bergen-Belsen, the [NON-ENGLISH] kept them self together because they were so few.
There weren't very many.
There were very few.
And I knew right away, I found out, [NON-ENGLISH], who was the [NON-ENGLISH] there.
And there was-- there were people that I knew from Brno.
They were together with me, my friends.
And Ilse was very young.
She was 10 1/2 years old when they arrested her.
And she hanged-- she survived by hanging to three girls that played in the orchestra in Auschwitz.
In the orchestra. you know, there was a big orchestra.
And then they were also deported to Bergen-Belsen.
And again, they kept together.
And they were all in one barrack.
And-- and she was the youngest.
She was only 15 then, I think.
Yeah, something like this. 16, 15 and 1/2.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was in charge of the DP camps.
And he had excellent conditions with the Red Cross.
And they got Red Cross packets that was worth a fortune.
Coffee, chocolate, sugar, candies.
You could trade it for goods, for food.
Yeah, yeah, anyway-- cigarette, anything.
And-- and when I was then, and I-- and I talked so fiercely and-- and emotionally that they should also come to kibbutz Buchenwald.
So we all go on earlier to Israel.
And when we went-- when I left there, she put in my knapsack all kind of food, chocolate and so.
That's when-- in Yiddish, she [YIDDISH].
You know what it means?
[YIDDISH]?
She started with me.
And then we went together on-- on-- on-- on illegal-- an illegal trip to Israel, to Palestine at that time.
Oh yeah.
She went with us illegally.
She also came to this kibbutz, Buchenwald, to the farm.
There was a concentration point where from all of Germany, they tried to find people that are willing.
Because there were certain risks involved.
To go illegally was not easy.
It was very difficult.
Some people went through Italy.
They march in the Alps in-- in-- in-- in frost and in cold weather, and people died.
That was a big undertaking.
You had to have a lot of courage to do that.
No, we were-- we are lucky that we made contact with the Jewish Brigade there was a unit in the British army.
And they took us through Holland, Belgium, and France.
We didn't go through Italy.
But it took us four months, five months.
A little bit.
No, not too often.
They took trucks from the-- from the British army.
And-- and they called us [GERMAN], that we are German prisoners, [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is to pigs, you know?
And they believed it.
We all were dressed up like German prisoners.
Because the British were watching too.
The British were on the outlook, that they knew or they suspected that this regiment of-- of Israeli-- of-- of Palestine [? sort ?] will-- will smuggle, which they did indeed.
They organized the [? brihad, ?] of course.
The illegal, [INAUDIBLE].
They organized us.
We arrived in March, '46.
The very first ship, [INAUDIBLE].
It was the very first ship that arrived.
They did.
They caught us on the high sea.
I have pictures of it.
They caught us on the high sea.
They-- they arrested us all and sent us to the internment camp, Atlit, near Haifa.
And we were three weeks in Atlit.
And since we were the first one, there was a tremendous PR going on.
Look what-- what-- the name was Bevin.
The Prime Minister, the anti-Semitic Prime Minister of England was Bevin.
And he said no, no, we have our agreement with the Arabs and so on and so.
Where it was such pressure, especially for the first one, the 1500, that they give special visas, special certificates.
And after three weeks, they released us.
And we were then distributed among the kibbutzim to get further training.
And after us, every ship that was caught, they was sent to Cyprus if you remember.
There were people two years in Cyprus after sitting in concentration camps, had to sit behind barbed wire in Cyprus.
It was terrible.
If I would have-- I-- I would have run away.
I would have done it.
To sit-- you know, at least-- I fight with the British.
When I was in Atlit, I called them, you-- son-of-a-bitches.
I provoked them.
One day they were running after me.
[LAUGHS]
Want to beat me up.
Because I couldn't stand-- Why were you angry with them?
And couldn't stand it. to be in a camp again.
Oh, we married in '49.
She was very young.
Yeah, two kids were--
Two kids were born in Israel, yeah.
In a kibbutz?
I was the-- they were born when we were in the kibbutz already.
I was the first two years, I was elected the general manager of the kibbutz.
I didn't--
Buchenwald.
It was-- we were stupid and we changed it.
They only-- they made fun of us.
They said, not enough there was a Buchenwald in Germany.
You had to bring it to Israel?
We changed it to Kibbutz Netzer then.
Between [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], but far from the [NON-ENGLISH].
It was actually between [NON-ENGLISH] and Ramla.
Ramla was-- was the front.
We were all in the army.
When the war broke out in [? '40, ?] everybody, the whole kibbutz were only women and children.
Oh yeah.
I-- I was first in [NON-ENGLISH].
That was one of the finest brigades, tell her.
It was one of the finest brigades, [NON-ENGLISH].
And then I was assigned to engineering corps.
Because I was in charge of the heavy equipment like tractors, bulldozers.
And then for-- for at least two months we were building anti-tank ditches around Tel Aviv.
Because there was-- there were two armored brigades of the Iraqis in Lod airport.
And Tel Aviv was constantly afraid that one night-- there was a main road, a good road to Tel Aviv.
And they could have taken the city.
Of course, they had equipment and we had nothing.
And the only way to stop them is to cut the-- the road on-- on-- on there. 50 points and cut it in half and build ditches so that-- that they couldn't pass.
And on the side, also were ditches where they couldn't pass.
That's what we did for two months.
Let me tell you how I came to America.
While I was in Israel, while I still was in the kibbutz, in 1956, suddenly I get a letter from the prosecutor-general.
They found-- look, the Germans left behind so many documentation that I was in Sachsenhausen And at that time, they prepared the trail of the murderers of the SS of Sachsenhausen.
They had a whole bunch of them arrested.
And they found me, that I live in Israel.
And they invite me to be a witness.
But the first thing, before I was a witness, they-- they-- they invited me to-- to a consul to identify some guys.
And at that time I still had a fantastic memory.
And I found them all.
I give them the names.
And then I became a witness.
And-- and in the trial I was three times.
The trial went on for years, for years.
Three times, I was in the Sachsenhausen trial.
Let me tell you something.
On the trial of Sachsenhausen, I was so good and I gave them a psychological profile of those mass murderers there.
There were four murderers there, but they're worse.
That-- that the president of the court asked me, are you a psychologist?
[LAUGHS] I said no, I have a terrific memory.
And then I-- the first time it was so good I became a professional witness.
Any time they had a trial with regard to Sachsenhausen, they invited me.
And later on they found out about us in Auschwitz.
I was three times in the Auschwitz trial.
So I was six times from Israel to Germany, to testify.
And the last time I was there, my brother and sister lived in America.
The rabbi and my sister.
My sister refused to go to Cyprus.
Otherwise she would have come to Israel too.
She said to go and to live two years in Cyprus?
No.
So they went to America.
And she was in a German quarter.
It was easy The German quarter were-- was not utilized.
You see?
She went fast.
And they brainwashed me.
I came here as a visitor.
And I said, you go to school.
You get yourself a profession.
So it took a year until my wife came with the children.
We spent a fortune on telephone calls and telegrams.
But eventually, I convinced her.
She didn't-- the first year, she wanted to go back.
She didn't want to stay here.
But now you tell her to go back, no way.
Yeah, yeah.
When I-- look, I first, right away, went to college.
OK?
I went to junior college because also I had no matriculation, but I went six years to-- to gymnasium.
We learned more than here with matriculation, believe me.
So I-- I went to the Jewish Federation.
They had a job-finding committee there.
No, here, here, here.
Yeah. yeah.
My-- my sister lived-- my brother lived in New York.
And he said, you go to live-- and they would be happier there than New York.
New York was-- was a mess then.
Anyway, so they find me a job in the garment industry.
The garment industry was, at that time, 99% Jewish.
So I started as nothing.
And after a year, I took the whole company.
[LAUGHS] And then, then I went to school and I made a Master's degree, an MBA.
And I joined a-- a very fashionable firm.
Ernst Strauss.
They made-- one of the finest suit company in the United States.
And then I became a partner.
And I bought the company.
Because they were-- they want to retire, the other two partners.
And then I, myself, retired, in '93.
I closed up.
Enough.
Yeah.
Look, let me tell you something.
After i went so many times to Germany to testify, I realize how important it is, that the world will know what happened.
At that time, nobody wanted to hear about the Holocaust.
I was in Germany in-- in the late '50s, and in the '60s.
The American Jewry was interested in-- in the world, in the Holocaust after the TV series.
Then, in the Eichmann Trial, that somehow woke up the world that the holocaust was just not a massacre.
It was a watershed event in human history.
And I got very active.
I was active in Israel too, in Holocaust causes.
Yeah.
There was an organization.
I was the Vice President.
And we, at that time, all we wanted, the people to testify.
Although they're-- they're-- there was no videotapes available, it was in the '60s, in the early-- no, in the '50s.
And when I came here I got involved very, very intimately in Holocaust causes.
In January, 1960.
I worked in the garment industry.
And I've went to school at the same time.
No, but I mean Jew-- in--
Oh, oh, oh, oh.
I got involved in the 1939 Club and got very active, involved.
I used to go-- years ago, I used to go to-- to-- to high schools and-- and-- and-- and tell about the holocaust.
And especially, I told you, after I was involved in-- in-- in testifying, I learned more.
And I used to teach people.
And then I got involved in-- in-- in-- also in-- in-- in the Hebrew University and [INAUDIBLE] University, Tel Aviv University.
I got involved in the Federation.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
The '39 Club is a fraternal organization of survivors of the Holocaust, primarily from Poland.
But there are also some German Jews and a few Hungarian Jews.
Especially if they're married to Polish Jews.
But it started with Polish Jews.
Now they-- they're in their late-- they started in '51 or something like this.
And later they realized that it-- it's not only Polish Jews.
They took anybody that was a survivor.
And they did a-- they did a very good job in-- in educating the public, in supporting a lot of causes in Israel.
And they were the first organization that established a chair for the study of the Holocaust at a public university.
You know that the UCLA has a Chair.
And that Chair is a-- a professor, a world-renowned scholar, Saul Friedlander.
And they are always oversubscribed.
I think the Chair exists only for 12 or 13 years.
They had an average of 250 to 300 students enrolled in those courses.
You know, there is a chance that some of those students will be the future leadership of the United States in the 21st century.
It's very important that people know about the Holocaust.
Most people that take that course are somewhat stimulated to read more.
And we already have sent-- we have a-- a program where we give a-- a stipend.
Holocaust-- not to [INAUDIBLE].
It's not a [INAUDIBLE].
We give $10,000 to a person that is willing to go for a PhD.
We already have created six PhD in Holocaust studies over the years.
Yeah.
And some of them are in-- in-- in Jerusalem.
Some of them are in small colleges.
And they teach teachers now, or students, you know?
Which is important.
You know, it's like almost the-- exponential.
It multiplies.
You know?
Thank you.
We'll go on on the next tape.
